    Early-Type Galaxies

    Early-Type Galaxies

    Type:Galaxy morphology class
    Level:Intermediate
    Subtypes:Ellipticals (E), lenticulars (S0)
    Related: Spiral galaxies
    Red sequence
    Quenching
    Galaxy mergers

    Early-type galaxies (ETGs) — ellipticals and lenticulars — are characterized by smooth, featureless morphologies, old stellar populations, red colors, and little or no ongoing star formation. They dominate the red sequence and are found preferentially in dense environments such as galaxy groups and clusters[1][2].

    Properties

    ETGs are pressure-supported systems dominated by random stellar motions rather than ordered rotation. They have high stellar mass surface densities, old mass-weighted ages (>5 Gyr typically), and high stellar metallicities reflecting extended early enrichment[2][3]. Their weak emission lines, when present, are powered by hot low-mass evolved stars (HOLMES) rather than young massive stars, earning them the designation "retired galaxies"[4][5].

    Formation Pathways

    ETGs are thought to form through a combination of mergers (which destroy disk structure and build spheroidal morphology) and quenching (which halts star formation)[1]. M51b (NGC 5195), the companion to M51a, exemplifies an ETG within an interacting pair: despite ongoing interaction with a star-forming spiral, M51b shows uniformly old populations and no significant star formation — a fully retired galaxy[6].

    See Also

    • Spiral galaxies
    • Star formation quenching
    • Red sequence / Green valley / Blue cloud
    • Thainá-Batista et al. (2026) — M51b as retired ETG

    References
    1. Cappellari, M. et al. (2016). "Structure and kinematics of early-type galaxies from ATLAS3D."
    2. Gallazzi, A. et al. (2005). "Ages and metallicities of galaxies in the local universe." MNRAS, 362, 41. DOI:10.1111/j.1365-2966.2005.09321.x
    3. Kauffmann, G. et al. (2004). "Environmental dependence of stellar mass, structure, SF." MNRAS, 353, 713. DOI:10.1111/j.1365-2966.2004.08117.x
    4. Stasińska, G. et al. (2008). "Retired galaxies: not quiescent, but powered by old stars." MNRAS.
    5. Cid Fernandes, R. et al. (2011). "True vs. fake AGN classification." MNRAS, 413, 1687. DOI:10.1111/j.1365-2966.2011.18244.x
    6. Thainá-Batista, J. et al. (2026). "Spatially resolved stellar populations in the M51 group with J-PLUS. II." A&A (accepted). arXiv:2603.15869
